normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: No explicit preconditions are required by the implementation; merge_sort is defined for all input lists/arrays.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (arr : List Int), WellFounded (measure (fun l => l.length)) (fun l => if l.length ≤ 1 then [] else [l.take (l.length/2), l.drop (l.length/2)])
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: The recursive calls in merge_sort strictly decrease the input length (split into two smaller halves), ensuring termination.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (left right : List Int), Sorted (merge left right)
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: The merge procedure outputs a sorted list when given two sorted input lists.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (left right : List Int), Permutation (merge left right) (left ++ right)
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: The merge procedure outputs a permutation of the concatenation of its inputs (it only reorders elements, does not create or drop elements).

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (left right : List Int), Sorted left → Sorted right → StableMerge left right (merge left right)
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Stability: when elements are equal, merge takes from the left first (because it uses <=), preserving relative order of equal elements across left/right.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (left right : List Int), (merge left right).length = left.length + right.length
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: The merged output length equals the sum of input lengths (elements are neither lost nor duplicated).
merge_sort returns a permutation of the input (same multiset of elements).
Stability of merge_sort: because it uses a stable merge at each step, the overall sort is stable.
merge_sort preserves length (no elements are added or removed).
